caveats: a: The occupational source covers all metal ore mining.
a: Task exposure is inferred rather than observed.
a: The supplied compensation ratio has a wage/receipts vintage mismatch and may be distorted by volatile metal prices.
rho: NIOSH evidence establishes active automation research and safety barriers, not realized labor substitution.
rho: Implementation economics differ sharply between open-pit and underground operations.
e: No source measures the eligible share directly.
e: Establishment classification does not reveal contract structure or firm ownership.
e: The supplied count is margin-bridged in a highly cyclical industry and may include explorers, subsidiaries, or temporarily profitable mines.
s5: The source is cross-industry, owner-level, and dated 2018.
s5: Property acquisitions are not necessarily qualifying firm control transfers.
s5: Owner age is less relevant for public, sponsor-owned, and joint-venture mines.
q: No source directly measures pass-through of AI-enabled mining savings.
q: Global commodity prices can dominate firm margins and obscure retention.
q: Cost reductions may extend mine life rather than appear as near-term retained cash benefit.
d5: BLS output is four-digit and includes other metals.
d5: End-use demand does not guarantee US mine production.
d5: Commodity price volatility complicates constant-price quantity inference.
o: The accountable entity may consolidate into a larger miner or contract operator.
o: Autonomous equipment reduces onsite labor but does not remove an accountable mine operator.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Copper, Nickel, Lead, and Zinc Mining

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Eligible firms earn repeat revenue under external operating, processing, tolling, dedicated offtake, or customer-specific supply arrangements; ordinary spot or merchant sale of ore from a firm's own resource is not by itself an outsourced service.
lens.excluded_activities: Mine-owning commodity producers lacking an outsourced-service relationship, exploration shells without operating revenue, captive mine units, royalty and financing vehicles, dormant properties, firms outside the EBITDA band, and standalone support contractors classified elsewhere.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms classified in copper, nickel, lead, or zinc mine development, extraction, and beneficiation that repeatedly provide externally contracted or customer-dedicated mining and concentrate supply operations.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment shares for four-digit metal ore mining, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in 212230. The measured mix includes 44.75% construction and extraction, 5.76% architecture and engineering, 3.87% science, 3.35% office support, and 4.97% transportation and material moving. I assign higher exposure to geology interpretation, scheduling, predictive maintenance, documentation, procurement, compliance, and dispatch analytics, and lower exposure to drilling, blasting, equipment operation, repair, sampling, and hazardous physical work. Gold and iron ore operations in the broader group may use different mine methods and automation, causing two-sided error.
caveats: The occupational source covers all metal ore mining.
Task exposure is inferred rather than observed.
The supplied compensation ratio has a wage/receipts vintage mismatch and may be distorted by volatile metal prices.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted occupational matrix and time study across geology, mine planning, dispatch, processing, maintenance, safety, administration, and mobile-equipment operation at eligible mines would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can improve orebody modeling, fleet and maintenance planning, processing optimization, safety analysis, reporting, procurement, and administrative work. Most employment is still tied to site-specific physical extraction, construction, haulage, maintenance, and accountable safety work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for four-digit metal ore mining to grow from $26.0 billion in 2024 to $33.5 billion in 2034, a 2.6% annual compound rate; five years at that rate is about 1.14. I use that as a quantity-demand proxy for 212230. DOE and USGS identify copper, nickel, lead, and zinc as critical minerals and note electrification-related demand, but the BLS group also includes gold, silver, and iron ore and domestic mine output is constrained by grades, permits, project timing, and imports, so direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: BLS output is four-digit and includes other metals.
End-use demand does not guarantee US mine production.
Commodity price volatility complicates constant-price quantity inference.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price US mine-production and offtake forecasts separately for copper, nickel, lead, and zinc, including operating-mine depletion, committed expansions, closures, grades, recovery, and permit probability, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Electrification, grid investment, vehicles, construction, batteries, and supply-security policy support base-metal demand. Domestic mine quantity can still stagnate if permitting delays, depletion, weak prices, imports, recycling, or project cancellations outweigh end-use growth.

caveats: No source measures the eligible share directly.
Establishment classification does not reveal contract structure or firm ownership.
The supplied count is margin-bridged in a highly cyclical industry and may include explorers, subsidiaries, or temporarily profitable mines.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the nine estimated LMM firms, including mine ownership, operating contracts, tolling revenue, offtake terms, external customers, property status, EBITDA quality, and captive relationships, would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: NAICS 212230 principally captures firms developing and mining their own ore bodies. Most sell commodity output rather than provide a recurring outsourced service, so they fail the frozen lens even when sales repeat. A minority may qualify through contract operation, toll beneficiation, customer-dedicated processing, or an economically service-like long-term arrangement; shells, captive units, and non-operating properties are excluded.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 212230 ($2,098,713,608) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 212230 ($16,700,960,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2936 (margins.json[21]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Metals & Mining EBITDA/Sales 29.36%); revenue/employee $1,034,628 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 212230); class <5: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,203,778 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $353,429 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,680,222 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,016,513 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $224,791,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,998,833 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,627,340,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $477,787,024 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 9

caveats: The accountable entity may consolidate into a larger miner or contract operator.
Autonomous equipment reduces onsite labor but does not remove an accountable mine operator.
o.change_evidence: Mine-plan and end-user evidence quantifying primary-metal demand displaced by recycling and recovery technologies, plus operating models for fully autonomous mines and third-party platform control, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five demand still requires accountable operators to control mineral rights or contracts, permits, drilling, blasting, haulage, beneficiation, tailings, reclamation, environmental compliance, and safety. AI and autonomy can change staffing and equipment control but cannot replace physical extraction and statutory operating accountability; recycling can substitute some primary material but belongs mainly in demand quantity.

caveats: No source directly measures pass-through of AI-enabled mining savings.
Global commodity prices can dominate firm margins and obscure retention.
Cost reductions may extend mine life rather than appear as near-term retained cash benefit.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm contracts showing cost-plus provisions, price escalators, treatment charges, royalties, gain-sharing, renewal cadence, and historical margin response to unit-cost reductions would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Fixed-price or market-linked ore sales can initially leave operating savings with the producer because metal prices are set globally rather than by disclosed mine cost. Over five years, service-contract renegotiation, offtake terms, royalties, wage bargaining, supplier pricing, depletion, declining grades, cost-curve competition, and commodity-price cycles share or absorb benefits.

caveats: NIOSH evidence establishes active automation research and safety barriers, not realized labor substitution.
Implementation economics differ sharply between open-pit and underground operations.
rho.change_evidence: Mine-level pre/post deployments showing labor hours, uptime, recovery, safety incidents, false alarms, operator adoption, integration cost, and sustained use across independent US base-metal mines would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Planning, document, monitoring, computer-vision, predictive-maintenance, and analytics use cases can scale within five years. Small firm size, remote connectivity, legacy equipment, mixed fleets, sparse labeled data, regulatory duties, union and workforce change, capital cycles, and safety validation constrain implementation, especially for autonomous physical operations.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of US employer businesses across industries were age 55 or older in 2018, not qualifying control transfers in base-metal mining. I discount heavily for public and institutional ownership, joint ventures, property-level rather than company-level deals, internal reorganizations, mine closures, permitting liabilities, commodity-cycle timing, failed transactions, and nontransferable operations. The all-industry age proxy likely overstates succession-driven transfers for capital-intensive mining firms.
caveats: The source is cross-industry, owner-level, and dated 2018.
Property acquisitions are not necessarily qualifying firm control transfers.
Owner age is less relevant for public, sponsor-owned, and joint-venture mines.
s5.change_evidence: S&P Capital IQ, PitchBook, or Mining Intelligence transactions mapped to US 212230 operating companies, with deal type, control, EBITDA, liabilities, and ownership age, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Mining assets do transact, but the very small eligible population, capital intensity, environmental liabilities, permitting, and prevalence of corporate or joint-venture structures reduce the likelihood of a qualifying five-year firm control transfer.

principal_driver: Electrification and critical-mineral demand support physical base-metal extraction and beneficiation.
principal_weakness: Most mine owners sell commodities rather than a recurring outsourced service, leaving very few verifiably eligible firms.
